metadata.name: WordPress Core Knowledge - Caching
metadata.description: Tests knowledge of WordPress caching APIs

tests.prompt: What function retrieves a transient value in WordPress?

choices.text: wp_get_transient()
get_transient()
fetch_transient()
transient_get()

tests.explanation: get_transient() retrieves the value of a transient.

tests.prompt: Which helper should you use to reuse WP_Query cache keys while still invalidating when last_changed updates in WordPress 6.9?

choices.text: wp_cache_flush_group()
wp_cache_get_salted() / wp_cache_set_salted()
Define WP_CACHE_KEY_SALT
Set cache_results to false on WP_Query

tests.explanation: New salted cache helpers embed last_changed into stored values so keys can be reused while stale data is rejected.

tests.prompt: When storing falsey values in an object cache group, how do you distinguish a cached false from a cache miss with WordPress 6.9 APIs?

choices.text: Check is_wp_error() on the return
Inspect the $found out parameter (or miss list in wp_cache_get_multiple*) to see if the key was present
Use wp_cache_exists()
Cache falsey values are not allowed; they are always misses

tests.explanation: WP_Object_Cache::get and wp_cache_get_multiple* report hits through the passed-by-reference $found flag/miss list, allowing false or 0 values to be distinguished from absent keys.

tests.prompt: When an empty string is passed as the cache group to wp_cache_set(), what group name is used internally?
tests.explanation: WP_Object_Cache methods check for empty group and replace it with 'default'. This is the implicit group for all cache operations that don't specify one.

tests.prompt: What is the key difference between wp_cache_add() and wp_cache_set()?

choices.text: wp_cache_add() fails if the key already exists; wp_cache_set() always overwrites
wp_cache_add() is persistent; wp_cache_set() is runtime-only
wp_cache_add() supports groups; wp_cache_set() does not
wp_cache_add() increments an existing value; wp_cache_set() replaces it

tests.explanation: wp_cache_add() returns false if the key already exists in the cache, making it safe for race conditions. wp_cache_set() unconditionally stores the value, overwriting any existing data.

tests.prompt: Without an external object cache, how are transients with an expiration stored in the database?

choices.text: A single row in a dedicated wp_transients table
Two options: _transient_{name} for the value and _transient_timeout_{name} for the expiration timestamp
A single option _transient_{name} containing both value and expiration in a serialized array
An entry in the wp_postmeta table tied to a special transients post

tests.explanation: Without an external cache, transients with a non-zero expiration are stored as two separate options in the wp_options table: one for the value and one for the timeout (time() + expiration). Both are stored as non-autoloaded options.

tests.prompt: What happens when you call set_transient() with an expiration of 0?

choices.text: The transient is not stored
The transient never expires and is stored as an autoloaded option (without external cache)
The transient expires immediately on the next request
It defaults to 1 hour

tests.explanation: An expiration of 0 means the transient never expires. Without an external cache, it's stored as an autoloaded option with no timeout option created. It persists until explicitly deleted.

tests.prompt: What is the filename of the drop-in file that WordPress looks for to load an external persistent object cache?
tests.explanation: WordPress checks for WP_CONTENT_DIR/object-cache.php. If this file exists and defines wp_cache_init(), WordPress sets wp_using_ext_object_cache(true) and uses it instead of the default WP_Object_Cache.

tests.prompt: When an external object cache is active, in which cache group are regular transients stored?
tests.explanation: With an external object cache, set_transient() stores data via wp_cache_set($transient, $value, 'transient', $expiration). Site transients use the 'site-transient' group instead.

tests.prompt: Which of these is a non-persistent cache group that WordPress core registers by default?

choices.text: counts
posts
options
users

tests.explanation: The 'counts' group is one of the non-persistent cache groups registered by WordPress core (along with 'plugins' and 'theme_json'). Non-persistent groups are never stored in an external persistent cache backend. Groups like 'posts', 'options', and 'users' are persistent by default.

tests.prompt: What value does wp_cache_get_last_changed() store as the 'last_changed' token?

choices.text: An auto-incrementing integer
The result of microtime() — a string with microseconds and Unix timestamp
A random UUID v4 string
The Unix timestamp from time()

tests.explanation: wp_cache_set_last_changed() calls microtime() and stores the result. The microtime string provides sufficient precision to detect rapid successive changes.

tests.prompt: When clean_post_cache() is called, how does it invalidate all cached WP_Query results?

choices.text: It calls wp_cache_flush_group('post-queries')
It deletes all cache keys matching a 'post-queries:*' pattern
It calls wp_cache_set_posts_last_changed() which updates the 'last_changed' token in the 'posts' group, causing salted cache reads to reject stale data
It iterates through all cached queries and deletes them individually

tests.explanation: clean_post_cache() calls wp_cache_set_posts_last_changed(), which updates the 'last_changed' key in the 'posts' cache group. Since WP_Query uses salted caching, stored results with the old salt no longer match and are effectively invalidated.

tests.prompt: In which WordPress version was wp_cache_flush_group() introduced?
tests.explanation: wp_cache_flush_group() was introduced in WordPress 6.1.0. The default WP_Object_Cache implementation simply unsets the entire group array from its internal $cache property.

tests.prompt: What is the maximum length of a transient name for set_transient()?
tests.explanation: Transient names must be 172 characters or fewer for set_transient(). Site transients have an even shorter limit of 167 characters. These limits account for the '_transient_' prefix within the options table's option_name column.

tests.prompt: The default WP_Object_Cache clones PHP objects when storing and retrieving them. Why?

choices.text: To ensure serialization compatibility with external backends
To prevent accidental mutation — modifications to a retrieved object won't affect the cached copy, and vice versa
To reduce memory usage via copy-on-write
To support multisite blog switching

tests.explanation: WP_Object_Cache clones objects on both set() and get() to prevent accidental mutation. Without cloning, modifying a retrieved object would also modify the cached copy since PHP objects are passed by reference.
